coredata.dc:title: UiO-66-NH2: An easily attainable and label-free turn-on probe for facile fluorescence sensing of alkaline phosphatase
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Fujian
Zhang, Jinyi
Zeng, Xiaoliang
Long, Zhou
Hou, Xiandeng
coredata.dc:description: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is now a well acknowledged indicator for diverse clinical diseases correlated with its abnormal expression. Compared to the assays by traditional and lately emerging methods, the fluorescent (FL) sensing, especially in the turn-on mode, has become more favored due to its simplicity, low cost, and high sensitivity and precision. However, the feasible FL probes are still limited and suffering from poor biological compatibility, complex preparation, additional modification or function-alization. Herein, we found that one common metal–organic framework, UiO-66-NH2 at nanoscale, could act as a new and label-free FL probe for facile sensing of ALP in the turn-on mode, based on the simple aid by phenyl phosphate which could be hydro-lyzed by ALP, with the product of phosphate ion enhancing the FL emission of UiO-66-NH2 via simple adsorption. Unlike many other FL probes in the turn-on mode, it was unnecessary to quench the FL of the probes in advance, greatly simplifying the sensor design. Moreover, by use of a new method based on dielectric barrier discharge (DBD), the UiO-66-NH2 could be prepared in 10 min, much shorter than that (usually hours) for preparing most other FL probes for ALP assay. The limit of detection was 0.4 U L-1, superior or at least rivalrous to the previously reported ones, and the ALP assay was also accomplished for real serum samples, with the analytical results highly consistent with those obtained by the clinical procedures, revealing its high practical utility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic framework
Alkaline phosphatase
Fluorescence
Label-free
Turn-on

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partially supported by 1•3•5 project for disciplines of excellence, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, China (No. ZY2016204), the Department of Science &Technology of Sichuan Province, China ( 2020YFH0105 ), and the Ministry of Education of China through the 111 Project, China (Grant No. B17030 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image (A), PXRD patterns (B), the excitation (C) and emission (D) scan of the UiO-66-NH2 probe.

The FL spectra of UiO-66-NH2 with the addition of Pi at different concentrations (A) and the according calibration curve (B); the variation in the FL intensity of UiO-66-NH2 caused by different species (C) and PP-ALP mixture in a time-evolving manner (D).
The FL decay curves (left) and absorption spectra (right).
Optimization of the concentration of PP (A) and UiO-66-NH2 (B); the change in FL intensity evolved with the ALP concentration (C) and the according calibration curve (D).
FL sensing of ALP by using UiO-66-NH2.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: a: The fluorescence decay was fitted to tri-exponential; b: The retrieved lifetime was calculated with the standard deviation as error.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results of ALP in human serum samples by this method and testing in hospital.
Comparison of ALP assays using different FL probes in turn-on mode.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: CD, carbon dot; GQD, graphene quantum dot; TPE, tetraphenylethylene; IM, if modified; N, no; Y, yes; ATP, adenosine triphosphate; PYP, pyrophosphate; AAP, ascorbic acid 2-phosphate; NPP, p-nitrophenyl phosphate; pG-DNA, 50-phosphoryl end of pG-rich DNA; and PTPE, phosphorylated tetraphenylethylene.

head.ce:title: UiO-66-NH2: An easily attainable and label-free turn-on probe for facile fluorescence sensing of alkaline phosphatase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environment, Southwest Minzu University, Chengdu 610041, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Environment, Southwest Minzu University, Chengdu 610041, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, and Key Lab of Green Chem & Tech (MOE), Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, and Key Lab of Green Chem & Tech (MOE)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, and Key Lab of Green Chem & Tech (MOE), Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is now a well acknowledged indicator for diverse clinical diseases correlated with its abnormal expression. Compared to the assays by traditional and lately emerging methods, the fluorescent (FL) sensing, especially in the turn-on mode, has become more favored due to its simplicity, low cost, and high sensitivity and precision. However, the feasible FL probes are still limited and suffering from poor biological compatibility, complex preparation, additional modification or function-alization. Herein, we found that one common metal–organic framework, UiO-66-NH2 at nanoscale, could act as a new and label-free FL probe for facile sensing of ALP in the turn-on mode, based on the simple aid by phenyl phosphate which could be hydro-lyzed by ALP, with the product of phosphate ion enhancing the FL emission of UiO-66-NH2 via simple adsorption. Unlike many other FL probes in the turn-on mode, it was unnecessary to quench the FL of the probes in advance, greatly simplifying the sensor design. Moreover, by use of a new method based on dielectric barrier discharge (DBD), the UiO-66-NH2 could be prepared in 10 min, much shorter than that (usually hours) for preparing most other FL probes for ALP assay. The limit of detection was 0.4 U L-1, superior or at least rivalrous to the previously reported ones, and the ALP assay was also accomplished for real serum samples, with the analytical results highly consistent with those obtained by the clinical procedures, revealing its high practical utility.

ce:section.ce:para: Biological enzymes are now widely regarded as the biomarkers for disease diagnosis by regulating metabolic pathways and physiological processes [1,2]. In particular, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is a subfamily of phosphatase enzymes widely distributed in cells, organs and tissues, and playing a vital role in biochemical behaviors as one of the most common indicators in routine clinical analysis and diagnostic procedures [3–5]. Abnormal ALP level in organisms is closely related to many diseases, and that in serum is more used as the reference for diagnostics than in other body fluids [6–8]. The normal ALP level in serum should be no less than 40 U L-1 [9], so accurate, sensitive and in-time ALP assay is highly significant.

The development of ALP assays is nowadays still at incipient stage, with electrochemistry [10] or Raman spectroscopy [11] as the most developed methods plus chemiluminescence [12] or chromatography [13] schemes. However, the drawbacks still remain in complex preparation or expensive instrumentation, so colorimetry and fluorescent (FL) sensing are lately attracting arising interests [5,8,9,14]. The former shows the advantages of simplicity but generally lacks sensitivity [9], while the latter has become a hotspot because of the high precision for the avoidance in tandem reactions, so the predominant progresses in ALP assay are now primarily based on FL strategies [8,9,15]. The so far established ones have been mostly achieved in the “turn-off” mode [8,9], but lately the turn-on strategy has become popular for better sensitivity, avoidance of false positive signals, and great potential in FL imaging [8,15]. A third “ratiometric” strategy could effectively avoid signal drift but with similar sensitivity to the other two, while more complicated mechanism and probe design could become the hindrance [14]. Therefore, the turn-on strategy is currently more favored for simple, fast, sensitive and selective ALP assays if highly demanded in urgency.

The major FL turn-on probes for ALP usually use noble metals [16], quantum dots [17], organic molecules [18] or polymers [19] as the FL probe, with necessary substrates except the organic molecules which, however, are limited for widespread applications due to complex preparations [8]. In general, the substrates should contain phosphate portions so that they could be hydrolyzed by ALP, with the obtained products especially phosphate ions (Pi) to induce further increase in FL intensity of the probes [8,9]. However, the drawbacks still remain in limited kinds of probes, poor biological compatibility, or additional modifications or FL quenching in advance, so the scope in applicable FL probes for ALP assays in the turn-on mode should be further expanded.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), as a class of crystalline porous materials [20] constructed with metallic nodes coordinated to organic ligands [21], have drawn widespread attentions in diverse fields [22–25] due to high porosity, exceptional tunability, structural diversity and robustness. The endeavors have never ceased for the exploration of their great potentials in new applications, and the catalytic activity or biocompatibility of MOFs has recently attracted arising interests in various applications including the ALP assays. However, MOFs have been employed mostly for colorimetric sensing of ALP as peroxidase mimics, while the FL features have rarely been taken advantage of. Despite the FL sensing for diverse target analytes [26–30] with various MOFs as the probes, the applications of in the assays of biomolecules are still hindered by the large size of the targets, less sensitive turn-off mode for the detections, or the additionally required FL labels [31–33]. In this context, inspired by the principles for ALP assays in the FL turn-on mode accomplished by the other kinds of probes, we found out that a common MOF, UiO-66-NH2 could be used as a label-free FL probe for the turn-on sensing of ALP, with phenyl phosphate (PP) as the substrate. As showed in Scheme 1 , ALP could easily hydrolyze the PP into phenol and phosphate ion (Pi), and as previously reported [34], the obtained Pi would easily cause the increase in the FL intensity of UiO-66-NH2 via simple adsorption. Moreover, the UiO-66-NH2 was easily attainable by using the new method based on dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) developed in our group recently [35], obtainable in as short as 10 min. It has also been validated that the MOFs prepared with this method are almost the same as those synthesized with the conventional ones, in terms of both micro-structures and optical properties [36]. It was further found that the FL intensity of the UiO-66-NH2 increased in a linear manner with the ALP concentration ranged from 0.5 to 15 U L-1, with a limit of detection of 0.4 U L-1. The ALP assay was also accomplished for serum samples, with the analytical results highly consistent with those obtained by standard clinical procedures, revealing the potential in practical utility of this new and simple technique.

PP (in the form of disodium hydrate) and ALP were from J&K Scientific and Shanghai Yuanye Biotech. Co., Ltd., China, respectively, and the others were from Aladdin Reagents Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), with all at least AR grade and used as received. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ∙cm) for all experiments here was produced by a purification system (PCWJ-10, Pure Technology Co. Ltd, Chengdu, China). The power X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns, FL data, absorption spectra, scanning electron microscope (SEM) images, and the FL lifetime were obtained from an EMPYREAN (Panalytical Inc., Netherlands, with a Cuκa radiation), a Hitachi F-7000 fluorometer (optical filter: 350 nm, excited at 327 nm), a UV-1750 UV/Vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), a JEOL JSM-7500F microscope, and a Fluorolog-3 fluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, excited at 327 nm with a ps photo detection module PPD-850), respectively.

The UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles (NPs) were obtained by using a newly developed DBD-based synthetic method in our group [35], which could be obtained in 10 min. For the ALP assay, 10 μL of the suspension (15 μg mL−1 in H2O) mixed with 50 μL of PP (4 mM) in 1 mL HEPES buffer (20 mM, pH = 8.0) was used as the sensing media, followed by the addition of ALP at 35 °C, with the FL data collected after 1 h. For real sample analysis, 5 μL of each human serum sample (from Sichuan Provincial People’s Hospital, Chengdu, China) instead of ALP was directly added into the same media, and the determined concentration of ALP was further compared with those obtained by using the reference procedure of Chinese professional standard (WS/T 351–2011).

As reported previously, the obtained UiO-66-NH2 NPs were sized in 100–300 nm (Fig. 1 A), with the PXRD patterns according well with the simulated [37] (Fig. 1B), and the best excitation and the highest emission at 320 and 425 nm, respectively (Fig. 1C&1D).

To preliminarily validate the proposed mechanism as shown in Scheme 1, we found out that, firstly, the FL intensity of UiO-66-NH2 could increase with the Pi concentration in a linear manner [34] (Fig. 2 A&2B); secondly, PP or ALP alone would cause negligible variation in the FL of UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. 2C), but a continuous increase of FL intensity was observed upon the addition of ALP to the UiO-66-NH2-PP mixture (Fig. 2D), primarily due to the hydrolysis of PP by ALP, with the product Pi causing the FL enhancement.

Furthermore, based on the thorough comparison with the previously reported FL probes for the assay of ALP in the turn-on mode (Table 3 ), it could be found that this developed technique herein have several advantages. Firstly, the UiO-66-NH2 could be easily obtained without any additional modification or functionalization, which could greatly shorten the preparation time and avoid any complex synthetic procedures only mastered by those with expertise. Secondly, only cheap precursors were needed for the preparation of UiO-66-NH2, but the yield was high, which could greatly decrease the expense by avoiding the usage of expensive biological or organic reagents. Thirdly, the sensor design was simple, especially compared with those requiring a turn-off step to quenching the FL of the probe in advance by introducing additional agents which might brought in possible interference, such as cation [39–41], metal oxide [42], organic molecule [43], or exonuclease [44]. Lastly, the sensitivity was superior or at least very rivalrous to the previously reported, with high practical utility.

In summary, a common and easily attainable MOF, UiO-66-NH2 was used as a label free FL probe for the turn-on sensing of ALP in a facile and sensitive manner. It shows the virtue of simplicity and low cost, together with the high sensitivity. Moreover, the performance in the analysis of serum samples was satisfactory. Therefore, this developed method shows promising perspectives in real sample analysis with practical utility.

Fujian Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jinyi Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Xiaoliang Zeng: Methodology, Investigation. Zhou Long: Methodology, Project administration. Xiandeng Hou: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by 1•3•5 project for disciplines of excellence, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, China (No. ZY2016204), the Department of Science &Technology of Sichuan Province, China (2020YFH0105), and the Ministry of Education of China through the 111 Project, China (Grant No. B17030).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gasotransmitter regulation of phosphatase activity in live cells studied by three-channel imaging correlation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Ou, R. Zhang, Z. Liu, X. Tian, G. Han, B. Liu, Z. Hu, Z. Zhang, Gasotransmitter Regulation of Phosphatase Activity in Live Cells Studied by Three-Channel Imaging Correlation, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., 58 (2019) 2261-2265.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Krzyzosiak, A. Sigurdardottir, L. Luh, M. Carrara, I. Das, K. Schneider, A. Bertolotti, Target-Based Discovery of an Inhibitor of the Regulatory Phosphatase PPP1R15B, Cell, 174 (2018) 1216-1228.
J.E. Coleman, Structure and Mechanism of Alkaline-phosphatase, Annual Review of Biophysics and Biomolecular Structure, 21 (1992) 441-483.
D.W. Moss, ALKALINE-PHOSPHATASE ISOENZYMES, Clin. Chem., 28 (1982) 2007-2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon Quantum Dots-Based Recyclable Real-Time Fluorescence Assay for Alkaline Phosphatase with Adenosine Triphosphate as Substrate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Qian, L. Chai, C. Tang, Y. Huang, J. Chen, H. Feng, Carbon Quantum Dots-Based Recyclable Real-Time Fluorescence Assay for Alkaline Phosphatase with Adenosine Triphosphate as Substrate, Anal. Chem., 87 (2015) 2966-2973.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.S. Qian, L.J. Chai, Y.Y. Huang, C. Tang, J. Jia Shen, J.R. Chen, H. Feng, A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots, Biosens. Bioelectron., 68 (2015) 675-680.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The chemical redox modulated switch-on fluorescence of carbon dots for probing alkaline phosphatase and its application in an immunoassay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Song, Q. Liu, Y. Zhang, W. Liu, M. Meng, Y. Yin, R. Xi, The chemical redox modulated switch-on fluorescence of carbon dots for probing alkaline phosphatase and its application in an immunoassay, RSC Advances, 8 (2018) 162-169.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel turn-on fluorescent detection of alkaline phosphatase based on green synthesized carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Qu, H. Pei, R. Kong, S. Zhu, L. Xia, Novel turn-on fluorescent detection of alkaline phosphatase based on green synthesized carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets, Talanta, 165 (2017) 136-142.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence turn-on sensing of ascorbic acid and alkaline phosphatase activity based on graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Huang, B. Wang, M. Chen, M. Liu, Y. Leng, X. Liu, Y. Li, Z. Liu, Fluorescence turn-on sensing of ascorbic acid and alkaline phosphatase activity based on graphene quantum dots, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 235 (2016) 356-361.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A turn-on fluorescence assay of alkaline phosphatase activity using a DNA–silver nanocluster probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Ma, H. Liu, K. Wu, M. Chen, H. He, K. Wang, K. Xia, A turn-on fluorescence assay of alkaline phosphatase activity using a DNA–silver nanocluster probe, New J. Chem., 42 (2018) 4331-4336.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence detection of alkaline phosphatase activity with [small beta]-cyclodextrin-modified quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Jia, J. Xu, D. Li, S.-P. Pang, Y. Fang, Z. Song, J. Ji, Fluorescence detection of alkaline phosphatase activity with [small beta]-cyclodextrin-modified quantum dots, Chem. Commun., 46 (2010) 7166-7168.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new fluorometric turn-on assay for alkaline phosphatase and inhibitor screening based on aggregation and deaggregation of tetraphenylethylene molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Gu, G. Zhang, Z. Wang, W. Liu, L. Xiao, D. Zhang, A new fluorometric turn-on assay for alkaline phosphatase and inhibitor screening based on aggregation and deaggregation of tetraphenylethylene molecules, Analyst, 138 (2013) 2427-2431.
